title: Expert Insights
description: The expert insights series presents perspectives on timely policy issues.
text:
Understanding China’s National Security Decisionmaking: A Primer for U.S. Policymakers
The authors provide a framework for describing the foundational elements that shape how governments make national security decisions—information, analysis, and authorities—and apply that framework to the People’s Republic of China.
Zachary Burdette, Shanshan Mei, Elizabeth Bujwid, Ian Burns McCaslin, Libby Weaver, Nathan Beauchamp-Mustafaga, Kristen Gunness
Pursuing Disarmament, Demobilization, and Reintegration in Gaza: A Critical Pathway to a Durable Peace
The authors propose a disarmament, demobilization, and reintegration (DDR) framework for Gaza. They also describe the preconditions necessary for a successful DDR program in Gaza, including the link to deradicalization.
Shira Efron, Charles P. Ries, Isabella Whitney, David E. Thaler
Measuring Biological Capabilities and Risks of AI Agents: Generating and Interpreting Evidence from Agentic Evaluations
This perspective examines biological agentic evaluations as a tool for assessing risks from increasingly autonomous AI systems and offers practical guidance on how evaluation design shapes conclusions about biological capability and risk.
Patricia Paskov, Jeffrey Lee, Kyle Brady, Alyssa Worland
Governing the Unknown: Insights from a RAND Workshop on Managing Polycrises and Catastrophic Risk
State and local governments face interconnected polycrises requiring new risk management approaches. Using workshop takeaways, this paper presents AI technologies, cross-regional partnerships, and insurance innovations as key adaptation strategies.
Joie D. Acosta, Sara Hughes, Jessica Jensen
Promising Practices for Building Stackable Credential Infrastructure
In this paper, the authors review promising practices for designing and implementing stackable credentials—a short-term credential that builds toward a higher-level credential—to inform the efforts of states and postsecondary institutions.
Jonah Kushner, Elaine W. Leigh, Phoebe Rose Levine, Melanie A. Zaber
Old priorities, new contexts: The institutional roots and new developments of China's rare earth policy
This perspective explores how China has adapted their rare earth elements (REE) policy to build a system that not only shapes global supply chains but also leverages global dependence on the critical materials.
Francesca Ghiretti, Conlan Ellis
Justice for the accused: Drivers and costs of inefficiencies in the criminal court system in England and Wales
The second in the Justice for All series, this paper reflects on the drivers and costs of inefficiencies in the criminal court system in England and Wales.
Emma Zürcher, Lucy Strang
Countering Russia-style autocracy in Georgia
The Georgia Dream party is embracing illiberalism, despite the pro-European aspirations of most Georgians. In this perspective, we trace this shift and offer options for increased Western support, to avoid a future of Russia-style autocracy.
William Dunbar, Harper Fine, Hanna Hodgetts, John Kennedy
A New Age of Nations: Power and Advantage in the AI Era
The author evaluates how artificial intelligence (AI) might affect the sources of national competitive advantage and proposes broad elements of a U.S. strategy for national competitive advantage in the AI Era.
Michael J. Mazarr
Scaling Pandemic Preparedness and Response Cost Estimates for Disease Transmission Modes
In this paper, the authors aim to understand what the world needs to spend annually to prepare for and respond to emerging respiratory pandemic diseases resulting from different modes of transmission: respiratory, insect-vectored, and fluid-borne.
Aaron Strong, Matt Sharkey, Saskia Popescu, Henry H. Willis, Anita Chandra
A Networked Leapfrog Strategy to Recapture Technology Leadership
Leapfrogging current-generation technologies to create entirely new paradigms may allow the United States to regain leadership in scientific and technological progress that is one of the foremost elements of the U.S.-China rivalry.
Michael J. Mazarr
